content.title: Choose by Automation Layer, Not by Language Preference
content.subtitle: 2026 Strategy: PowerShell, TypeScript, and RPA Architecture
content.message: The right decision is not one standard tool, but one standard selection model. Use PowerShell for the OS, TypeScript for the platform, and RPA for legacy gaps.

content.title: Comparative Analysis: Tactical Alternatives
points: Decision Rule #1: PowerShell for OS-level boundaries
Decision Rule #2: TypeScript for platform-level boundaries
Decision Rule #3: RPA as a necessary legacy bridge
Recommended Operating Model and Final Policy

content.title: Problem: One Standard Creates Wrong Tradeoffs
content.subtitle: A single-tool strategy increases automation debt
keyPoints: PowerShell overreach: Using scripts for API-heavy products creates maintainability problems.
TypeScript overreach: Using cloud stacks for simple file operations adds unnecessary overhead.
RPA overreach: Using bots where APIs exist creates brittle and expensive automation.
Overstandardization causes teams to use strong tools in weak-fit situations.

content.title: Solution #1: PowerShell for the Operating System
content.subtitle: Best fit for file handling and shell execution
keyPoints: Default choice for OS-level automation, file ingestion, and validation.
Handles rename/move/archive operations and scheduled batch jobs natively.
Ideal for server administration and shell command orchestration.
PowerShell 7.5 focus: Quality, security, and stability of the platform.

content.title: Solution #2: TypeScript for the Platform
content.subtitle: Best fit for BTP extensions and modern integration logic
keyPoints: Strategic choice for SAP BTP, CAP services, and API-driven workflows.
Ideal for maintainable multi-developer codebases and event handling.
Native support for Node.js service layers and UI extension components.
TypeScript has become the preferred mode for enterprise-scale engineering.

content.title: Solution #3: RPA as the Necessary Bridge
content.subtitle: Use only when screens are the only available interface
keyPoints: Justified when no stable API exists and legacy apps cannot be changed.
Acts as a bridge for GUI-only workflows to modern downstream systems.
Market demand remains high due to persistent legacy interface gaps.
Evolving from simple screen scraping to governed automation platforms.

content.title: Operating Model: API First, Script Second, RPA Last
content.subtitle: A practical decision tree for enterprise teams
keyPoints: Priority 1: If stable API/Event exists, use JavaScript/TypeScript on BTP.
Priority 2: If primarily an OS/file/system task, use PowerShell.
Priority 3: If only interface is a GUI, use governed Enterprise RPA.
A repeatable sequence prevents subjective and brittle tooling debates.

content.title: Market Evidence: TypeScript Dominance
content.subtitle: Exclusive usage continues to rise in modern work

content.title: Market Evidence: RPA Still Growing
content.subtitle: Expansion remains real as categories evolve

content.title: Infrastructure View: Reliability Matters
content.subtitle: Operational stability as the core requirement

quote.text: For this release the focus has been on quality, security and stability of the platform.
quote.speaker: Jason Helmick
quote.title: Senior Product Manager, PowerShell Team

content.title: Final Recommendation: Three Tiers, Three Owners
body: PowerShell: Operates the estate (Files, Admin, Jobs). Owned by IT Ops.
JavaScript/TypeScript: Extends the platform (BTP, APIs). Owned by Engineering.
Enterprise RPA: Bridges the legacy gap (GUI-only). Owned by Automation CoE.
Policy: Standardize the decision model, not a single language.
KPI: Target 80%+ VBScript retirement by the end of 2026.
